Level 1: you have used 101 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 101 actions returned the board to a state already seen 101 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 101 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game is over, waiting for auto-reset. Let me check if we're already reset.
print("State:", last_action_result.get('state'))
print("Game over:", last_action_result.get('game_over'))
print("Level:", current_frame.level)

# Check if the board looks like the initial state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 50 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Try an action to see if game resets
action(['LEFT'])
print("\nAfter LEFT:")
print("Level:", current_frame.level)
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))
print("Game over:", last_action_result.get('game_over'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 50 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
